evidence: ComparisonResult.java lines 11-14: explicit Javadoc states 'This is a traditional class (not a record), so accessor methods intentionally use the get prefix following standard JavaBeans conventions. Record types in this package omit the get prefix per Java record conventions.'

ExplorationResult.java line 11: declared as 'public class ExplorationResult' — a traditional class — but carries no corresponding rationale comment; its accessors (getSeed(), getSeedCollocates(), getDiscoveredNouns(), etc.) use get prefix without explanation

model/ package: 5 records (AdjectiveProfile, CoreCollocate, DiscoveredNoun, Edge, ExploreOptions) use component accessor names; 2 classes (ComparisonResult, ExplorationResult) use get-prefixed accessors — half the convention contract is documented, half is not

utils/: CqlUtils, LogDiceCalculator, LongIntHashMap all follow the identical static-utility pattern (final or plain class, private constructor, all static methods) — fully consistent across the package
api/ handler classes SketchHandlers and ExplorationHandlers are both package-private with package-private methods — consistent scoping throughout
All packages use standard Java camelCase for methods/variables, PascalCase for types, UPPER_SNAKE_CASE for constants — no cross-package naming drift detected
QueryResults sealed interface applies the record/interface bridge pattern identically across all three ConcordanceResult subtypes (SnippetResult, KwicResult, CollocateResult)
convention_outlier.issues_preventing_higher_score: ExplorationResult in the model package does not carry the class-vs-record rationale comment that ComparisonResult documents explicitly. A developer reading ExplorationResult first sees get-prefixed accessors without understanding why it diverges from the record siblings; they must find ComparisonResult's Javadoc to learn the convention is intentional.

strengths: All packages use uniform Java naming conventions throughout — camelCase methods/variables, PascalCase types, UPPER_SNAKE_CASE constants — with no cross-package naming drift
ComparisonResult explicitly documents the class-vs-record type strategy in its Javadoc with accurate cross-references to the record siblings, demonstrating deliberate convention management
The utils/ static-utility trio (CqlUtils, LogDiceCalculator, LongIntHashMap) apply the same pattern uniformly: final/plain class, private constructor, all static methods
Handler classes SketchHandlers and ExplorationHandlers follow a consistent package-private visibility pattern, correctly scoped as internal api/ collaborators
QueryResults sealed interface applies the record/interface bridge pattern identically across all three ConcordanceResult subtypes
convention_outlier.issue_character: The single issue is a documentation gap rather than structural convention drift — the underlying class-vs-record split in model/ is applied correctly and intentionally, but only one of the two classes (ComparisonResult) carries the Javadoc rationale that makes the convention self-evident.
convention_outlier.score_rationale: The codebase is strongly consistent at the naming, package organisation, and behavioral protocol level; all packages share the same Java naming idioms and all sibling modules follow the same structural protocols. Against the 90-anchor ('mostly consistent with minor style islands that don't cause real confusion'), the only friction is ExplorationResult lacking the class-vs-record rationale comment its sibling ComparisonResult provides — a developer would briefly encounter confusion when seeing mixed get/component accessor styles in the model package. This is not a noticeable drift pattern (80-anchor territory) but it is a small, fixable gap that prevents a clean 90.

issues.summary: ExplorationResult lacks the class-vs-record rationale Javadoc that ComparisonResult provides, leaving half the model convention undocumented
evidence: ComparisonResult.java lines 11-14 document: 'This is a traditional class (not a record), so accessor methods intentionally use the get prefix following standard JavaBeans conventions. Record types in this package omit the get prefix per Java record conventions.'

ExplorationResult.java is also a traditional class with get-prefixed accessors (getSeed(), getSeedCollocates(), getDiscoveredNouns(), getSeedCollocateFrequencies(), getCoreCollocates()) but carries no corresponding rationale comment

A developer reading ExplorationResult before ComparisonResult sees mixed get/component accessor conventions in the model package without an explanation — DiscoveredNoun uses noun(), sharedCount(), avgLogDice() while ExplorationResult uses getSeed(), getDiscoveredNouns()
issues.suggestion: Add to ExplorationResult's class-level Javadoc: 'This is a traditional class (not a record), so accessor methods intentionally use the {@code get} prefix following standard JavaBeans conventions. Record types in this package (e.g. {@link AdjectiveProfile}, {@link CoreCollocate}, {@link DiscoveredNoun}) omit the {@code get} prefix per Java record conventions.' — mirroring the rationale already present on ComparisonResult.

root_causes: The class-vs-record rationale was documented when ComparisonResult was written (or later added) but not propagated to ExplorationResult, which was likely written at a different time
